text: These are the chips that will launch the next AI era. Number 14, Nvidia Reuben. Reuben is Nvidia's upcoming architecture that's expected in 2026, and it's been one of the biggest topics in the AI hardware world. Jensen Hang confirmed that Reuben will support HBM4 and move to more advanced process nodes. Industry reports suggest it may target around 1.2 two exof flops in FP8 workloads, which would be a significant jump from Blackwell. Whether AI reaches artificial general intelligence or not, Reuben is part of the infrastructure that makes extremely large-scale training viable. It's basically the next step in Nvidia's long-term road map for large model compute. Number 13, Google TPU V6 Ironwood. Google designed Ironwood to train the newest Gemini models across huge clusters. Google has publicly stated that TPUVP5 increased training efficiency by more than two times compared to earlier versions and Ironwood continues that trend with higher throughput and better energy efficiency. Google highlighted that Ironwood is optimized for large multimodal systems that use video, audio, and long context reasoning. The real power comes from the pod scale where thousands of TPUs work together. Google uses these pods to train its Frontier models faster and at lower energy per training step than before. Number 12, Nvidia Blackwell B200. Blackwell is Nvidia's flagship 2024 architecture and it comes with major improvements like up to 288 GB of HBM3e memory and significantly higher FP4 and FP8 performance. Nvidia reported that Blackwell can deliver around 20 pedlops of FP4 compute per GPU and over,300 GB per second of memory bandwidth. Companies are already using Blackwell for training advanced language models, vision language models, and AI agents that require real time reasoning. The biggest advantage of Blackwell is efficiency since it offers large gains in performance per watt and lowers the cost of training and inference at massive scales. Number 11, AMD MI400 series. AMD revealed the MI400 class accelerators will use the next generation CDNA architecture and HBM4 memory. While full specs aren't released yet, AMD has confirmed that MI400 is built for extremely high bandwidth workloads which are critical for large model training. AMD's been gaining momentum with its MI300 series, which powers several public LLM deployments, and MI400 continues that direction with better interconnects and higher efficiency. This positions AMD as a meaningful alternative for cloud providers that want diverse suppliers for AI compute. The competition here matters because more hardware options push faster innovation. Number 10, Tesla Dojo V2. Dojo is Tesla's custom supercomput built mainly for training its autonomous driving system. Tesla released details showing that Dojo uses custom D1 chips arranged on large training tiles that scale across thousands of units. The system aims for extremely high throughput video training since Tesla collects billions of frames from its fleet. Dojo V2 improves on the first generation with better thermal performance and updated compute tiles. Even if Dojo specialized, it represents a new trend where companies build dedicated hardware for their own AI

system aims for extremely high throughput video training since Tesla collects billions of frames from its fleet. Dojo V2 improves on the first generation with better thermal performance and updated compute tiles. Even if Dojo specialized, it represents a new trend where companies build dedicated hardware for their own AI generalpurpose accelerators. Number nine, Intel Falcon Shores. Falcon Shores is Intel's upcoming XPU designed to bring CPU and GPU style compute into one architecture. Intel confirmed that Falco Shores will support extremely high HBM capacities and chipletbased modularity. The company's positioning it for largecale AI training where bandwidth and memory density matter as much as raw flops. Intel has stated publicly that Falcon Shores aims to catch up in the generative AI market by focusing on efficiency and predictive scaling. Several cloud providers have already partnered with Intel to evaluate Falcon Shores for future AI clusters since diversification in compute supply is becoming a priority. Before we move on, a quick note that might actually be useful if you create content yourself. There's a new tool called Overseer OS that analyzes YouTube channels and breaks down what works across videos, formats, and posting patterns. It basically reverse engineers channels so you can study real examples instead of guessing. If you're curious, the links in the description. Number eight, AWS Tranium 2. AWS unveiled Tranium 2 at Invent 2023, describing it as four times faster in training performance compared to the first generation. Tranium 2 is built for large model workloads that run across thousands of accelerators in AWS ultracluster setups. AWS said that future models with hundreds of billions of parameters will be trained on clusters powered by Tranium 2 and that these chips offer better performance per watt for long training cycles. Since AWS hosts a significant share of generative AI development, Tranium 2 helps reduce dependency on external chip suppliers and gives developers another pathway to train advanced models at scale. Number seven, Cerebrris W3. Cerebras takes a different approach by building an entire silicone wafer into one massive chip instead of cutting it into smaller dieses. The WSE3 is built on TSMC's 5 nanometer process and contains around 4 trillion transistors with 900,000 compute cores. Cerebrus says WSE3 improves training throughput and lowers energy per token compared to previous generations. The goal is to remove the communication bottlenecks that happen across large GPU clusters. Several national labs and research teams use WSSE systems for scientific workloads and large language model experiments that benefit from faster iteration without complex distributed setups. Number six, Grock LPU. Grock focuses on extreme low latency inference rather than large-scale training. The company publicly demonstrated its LPU system serving large language models at speeds that far exceed traditional GPUbased inference. Gro's architecture is built on deterministic data flow which makes throughput and latency predictable something valuable for real-time applications like robotics, conversational agents and financial systems. The system shows how future AI won't only depend on training speed but

large language models at speeds that far exceed traditional GPUbased inference. Gro's architecture is built on deterministic data flow which makes throughput and latency predictable something valuable for real-time applications like robotics, conversational agents and financial systems. The system shows how future AI won't only depend on training speed but instantaneous responses. This is important as models with longer context and more complex reasoning continue to grow. Number five, Samanova SN40L. Samanova designed the SN40L architecture for enterprisecale AI inference. It uses a data flow execution model that optimizes the path each operation takes through the hardware. SambaNova claims that this makes inference more efficient for large models that companies deploy privately. The system supports full parameter models instead of quantized or reduced versions which is useful for accuracy sensitive workloads like medical or financial analysis. Several organizations have reported cost reductions when switching to sambova systems because of lower energy requirements. It shows how the AI compute landscape isn't driven only by training hardware, but also by efficient deployment solutions. Number four, AMD MI350. AMD positioned the MI350 series as the bridge between today's accelerators in the next generation MI400 lineup. MI350 uses the CDNA4 architecture and is designed for FP4 and FP8 workloads that are common in modern language models. AMD shared that MI350 delivers a large performance gain over MI300, especially in energy efficiency. Cloud providers have started integrating MI300 hardware into their platforms, which means MI350 will likely appear in similar environments once released. It shows how the AI hardware market is expanding beyond a single dominant supplier. Something many companies have asked for as demand keeps rising. Number three, AWS Inferentia 2. Inferentiate 2 powers large-scale inference on AWS and is designed to serve models like Llama, Claude, and other highparameter systems. AWS stated that Inferentia 2 offers higher throughput and lower latency than the first generation with better price performance for longunning inference workloads. It can handle large batch sizes and supports advanced quantization formats that reduce memory usage without a major loss in accuracy. companies use inferentiabased instances to deploy language models in production since it reduces overall serving costs. It highlights how inference hardware is becoming just as important as training hardware. Number two, Tentorant, wormhole, and black hole. Ten torrent builds rivbbased AI processors led by semiconductor architect Jim Keller who previously worked on major chip designs at AMD, Apple, and Tesla. Tentor Torrent focuses on scalable mesh networking in efficient data movement so systems can run large models more predictably. The company has shared that its upcoming products including wormhole and black hole are built to serve both training and inference workloads depending on the configuration. Several automotive and robotics companies have partnered with tentor torrent to explore how these chips can support realtime decision-making. It's one of the newer players aiming to diversify global AI compute. Number one, Huawei Ascend 910B. Huawei's Ascend 910B is one of China's most advanced AI accelerators and is

configuration. Several automotive and robotics companies have partnered with tentor torrent to explore how these chips can support realtime decision-making. It's one of the newer players aiming to diversify global AI compute. Number one, Huawei Ascend 910B. Huawei's Ascend 910B is one of China's most advanced AI accelerators and is large data centers. While Huawei does not disclose all performance details publicly, Chinese research labs have reported strong performance in FP16 compute and high bandwidth memory throughput. The Ascend lineup is used in a wide range of national AI projects, including language model training and scientific computing. It reflects how countries are building their own high-performance hardware is part of broader efforts to strengthen domestic AI capabilities. This trend has become more visible as global demand for compute continues to grow. The new generation AI chips is built to handle training workloads that were impossible a few years ago. They support larger multimodal models, faster reasoning, and real time decision-making across robotics, simulation, and scientific research. This level of compute lets researchers experiment with architectures that move closer to AGI, where models can understand, plan, and adapt across different tasks. Some scientists also study how scaling laws might eventually push systems towards super intelligent behavior. although that remains theoretical. What is clear is that these chips make far more advanced AI development feasible at a global scale. If you made it this far, let me know what you think in the comments. title: The Chip That Will Launch the Next AI Era
